content:
# Titus Chapter 2 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### Verse 1
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "speak the things which are fitting to the sound teaching" |
| LOGOS | "speak the things that are fitting for sound teaching" |
| PNEUMA | "teach what is consistent with sound doctrine" |
| **SOPHIA** | "speak the things that are fitting for sound teaching" |

**Analysis:** EMET and LOGOS both preserve the verb ΛΑΛΕΙ ("speak"), while PNEUMA paraphrases as "teach." EMET includes the definite article ("the sound teaching") following the Greek article ΤΗ; LOGOS drops it for smoother English; PNEUMA renders as "sound doctrine." SOPHIA follows LOGOS, retaining the verb "speak" and the natural English phrasing without the article.

---

### Verse 2
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Old men to be sober-minded, dignified, self-controlled, being-sound in the faith, in the love, in the endurance" |
| LOGOS | "that older men be sober-minded, dignified, self-controlled, sound in faith, in love, in endurance" |
| PNEUMA | "Older men should be sober-minded, worthy of respect, and self-controlled — solid in their faith, their love, and their endurance" |
| **SOPHIA** | "Older men are to be sober-minded, dignified, self-controlled, sound in faith, in love, in endurance" |

**Analysis:** PNEUMA renders ΣΕΜΝΟΥΣ as "worthy of respect" rather than "dignified" (EMET/LOGOS). PNEUMA also uses "solid" for ΥΓΙΑΙΝΟΝΤΑΣ where EMET has "being-sound" and LOGOS has "sound." EMET retains the Greek articles ("the faith, the love, the endurance"); LOGOS and PNEUMA drop them. SOPHIA follows the EMET/LOGOS agreement on "dignified" and "sound" while adopting natural English.

---

### Verse 3
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Old women likewise in deportment reverent, not slanderers, not having-been-enslaved to much wine, teachers-of-what-is-good" |
| LOGOS | "likewise older women, in deportment befitting holiness, not slanderers, not enslaved to much wine, teachers of what is good" |
| PNEUMA | "In the same way, older women should live in a way that honors God. They must not be slanderers or slaves to heavy drinking. Instead, they should teach what is good" |
| **SOPHIA** | "Older women likewise are to be reverent in deportment, not slanderers, not enslaved to much wine, teachers of what is good" |

**Analysis:** The key divergence is on ΙΕΡΟΠΡΕΠΕΙΣ: EMET renders "reverent," LOGOS renders "befitting holiness" (preserving the sacred/temple connotation), and PNEUMA paraphrases as "live in a way that honors God." PNEUMA also renders "much wine" as "heavy drinking" and restructures the verse into multiple sentences. SOPHIA uses "reverent" (EMET) as a concise rendering that captures the sense while noting LOGOS's fuller explanation in the notes.

---

### Verse 4
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "so-that they-might-train-the-young-women to-be lovers-of-[their]-husbands, lovers-of-[their]-children" |
| LOGOS | "so that they may train the young women to be lovers of their husbands, lovers of their children" |
| PNEUMA | "so that they can train the younger women to love their husbands and their children" |
| **SOPHIA** | "so that they may train the young women to love their husbands, to love their children" |

**Analysis:** EMET and LOGOS preserve the compound adjectives ΦΙΛΑΝΔΡΟΥΣ and ΦΙΛΟΤΕΚΝΟΥΣ as "lovers of their husbands/children." PNEUMA simplifies to verbal infinitives "to love their husbands and their children." SOPHIA uses the verbal form for readability while preserving the parallel structure with repetition of "to love."

---

### Verse 5
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "self-controlled, pure, workers-at-home, good, being-submitted to their own husbands, so-that the word of God might not be blasphemed" |
| LOGOS | "self-controlled, pure, workers at home, good, being subject to their own husbands, so that the word of God may not be reviled" |
| PNEUMA | "to be self-controlled and pure, to manage their households well, to be kind, and to submit to their own husbands, so that no one will be able to speak against God's message" |
| **SOPHIA** | "to be self-controlled, pure, workers at home, good, submitting to their own husbands, so that the word of God may not be reviled" |

**Analysis:** Multiple divergences. (1) ΟΙΚΟΥΡΓΟΥΣ: EMET/LOGOS agree on "workers at home"; PNEUMA paraphrases as "manage their households well." (2) ΑΓΑΘΑΣ: EMET/LOGOS render "good"; PNEUMA renders "kind." (3) The final clause: EMET has "blasphemed" (most literal for ΒΛΑΣΦΗΜΗΤΑΙ), LOGOS has "reviled," PNEUMA paraphrases entirely. SOPHIA follows the EMET/LOGOS agreement on "workers at home" and "good," and adopts LOGOS's "reviled" as an accurate and readable rendering.

---

### Verse 7
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "concerning all things presenting yourself [as] a pattern of good works, in the teaching [showing] incorruptness, dignity" |
| LOGOS | "in all things, showing yourself a pattern of good works; in your teaching showing integrity, dignity" |
| PNEUMA | "And you yourself must be an example to them by doing good works of every kind. Let everything you teach be done with integrity and seriousness" |
| **SOPHIA** | "in all things showing yourself as a pattern of good works; in your teaching showing integrity, dignity" |

**Analysis:** (1) ΑΦΘΟΡΙΑΝ: EMET renders "incorruptness" (most literal), LOGOS renders "integrity," PNEUMA renders "integrity." SOPHIA follows the LOGOS/PNEUMA agreement. (2) ΣΕΜΝΟΤΗΤΑ: EMET has "dignity," LOGOS has "dignity," PNEUMA has "seriousness." SOPHIA follows the EMET/LOGOS agreement. (3) PNEUMA substantially restructures the verse into two separate sentences and replaces "pattern" with "example."

---

### Verse 8
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "sound speech [that is] beyond-reproach, so-that the [one] from the opposing [side] might-be-ashamed, having nothing evil to say concerning us" |
| LOGOS | "sound speech that is beyond reproach, so that the one who opposes may be put to shame, having nothing evil to say about us" |
| PNEUMA | "Let your message be so sound that it cannot be criticized, so that anyone who opposes you will be put to shame because there is nothing bad to say about us" |
| **SOPHIA** | "sound speech that is beyond reproach, so that the one who opposes may be put to shame, having nothing evil to say about us" |

**Analysis:** EMET and LOGOS closely agree. PNEUMA paraphrases significantly: "message" for "speech," "cannot be criticized" for "beyond reproach," "nothing bad" for "nothing evil." All three correctly follow Sinaiticus reading ΗΜΩΝ ("us") rather than the variant ΥΜΩΝ ("you"). SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS agreement.

---

### Verse 9
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Slaves to their own masters to-be-submitted in all things, to-be well-pleasing, not speaking-against" |
| LOGOS | "Bondservants are to be subject to their own masters in all things, to be well-pleasing, not contradicting" |
| PNEUMA | "Slaves must submit to their masters in everything and try to please them. They must not talk back" |
| **SOPHIA** | "Bondservants are to be subject to their own masters in all things, seeking to be well-pleasing, not contradicting" |

**Analysis:** (1) ΔΟΥΛΟΥΣ: EMET renders "Slaves," LOGOS renders "Bondservants," PNEUMA renders "Slaves." SOPHIA adopts "Bondservants" (LOGOS) as more precise for the ancient institution. (2) ΑΝΤΙΛΕΓΟΝΤΑΣ: EMET has "speaking-against," LOGOS has "contradicting," PNEUMA has "talk back." SOPHIA follows LOGOS. (3) PNEUMA omits "their own" (ΙΔΙΟΙΣ) and restructures into multiple sentences.

---

### Verse 10
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "not pilfering, but showing all good faithfulness, so-that they-might-adorn the teaching, the [teaching] of the Savior of-us, God, in all things" |
| LOGOS | "not pilfering, but showing all good faithfulness, so that they may adorn the teaching of God our Savior in all things" |
| PNEUMA | "or steal from them. Instead, they must show complete and genuine faithfulness in everything, so that in every way they will make the teaching about God our Savior attractive" |
| **SOPHIA** | "not pilfering, but showing all good faithfulness, so that they may adorn the teaching of God our Savior in all things" |

**Analysis:** (1) ΝΟΣΦΙΖΟΜΕΝΟΥΣ: EMET/LOGOS agree on "pilfering"; PNEUMA renders "steal." (2) "All good faithfulness" (EMET/LOGOS) vs. "complete and genuine faithfulness" (PNEUMA). (3) ΚΟΣΜΩΣΙΝ: EMET/LOGOS render "adorn"; PNEUMA renders "make attractive." (4) Word order of "Savior/God": EMET preserves Greek word order "the Savior of-us, God"; LOGOS/PNEUMA render "God our Savior." SOPHIA follows LOGOS.

---

### Verse 11
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "For the grace of God has-appeared, saving to all men" |
| LOGOS | "For the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation to all people" |
| PNEUMA | "For the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation to all people" |
| **SOPHIA** | "For the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation to all people" |

**Analysis:** EMET preserves the adjectival form ΣΩΤΗΡΙΟΣ literally as "saving" and retains "men" (ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΙΣ). LOGOS and PNEUMA both render "bringing salvation" (making the adjective into a participial phrase) and use the inclusive "people." SOPHIA follows the LOGOS/PNEUMA agreement, which clearly conveys the meaning while using inclusive language.

---

### Verse 12
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "training us, so-that having-denied the ungodliness and the worldly desires, self-controlledly and righteously and godly we-might-live in the present age" |
| LOGOS | "training us, so that, having denied ungodliness and worldly desires, we might live self-controlled and righteously and godly in the present age" |
| PNEUMA | "It trains us to reject godless living and worldly desires, and to live in this present age with self-control, integrity, and devotion to God" |
| **SOPHIA** | "training us, so that, having denied ungodliness and worldly desires, we might live self-controlled and righteously and godly in the present age" |

**Analysis:** (1) EMET/LOGOS preserve the participial structure "having denied" (ΑΡΝΗΣΑΜΕΝΟΙ); PNEUMA converts to infinitive "to reject." (2) The three adverbs ΣΩΦΡΟΝΩΣ ΚΑΙ ΔΙΚΑΙΩΣ ΚΑΙ ΕΥΣΕΒΩΣ: EMET renders "self-controlledly and righteously and godly"; LOGOS renders "self-controlled and righteously and godly"; PNEUMA renders "with self-control, integrity, and devotion to God." PNEUMA's "integrity" for ΔΙΚΑΙΩΣ ("righteously") and "devotion to God" for ΕΥΣΕΒΩΣ ("godly") are interpretive departures. (3) PNEUMA adds "this" before "present age." SOPHIA follows LOGOS.

---

### Verse 14
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "who gave himself on-behalf-of us, so-that he-might-redeem us from all lawlessness and might-purify for-himself a people [for his] own-possession, zealous of good works" |
| LOGOS | "who gave himself for us, that he might redeem us from all lawlessness and purify for himself a people for his own possession, zealous for good works" |
| PNEUMA | "He gave himself for us to set us free from every kind of lawlessness and to purify for himself a people who are his very own, eager to do what is good" |
| **SOPHIA** | "who gave himself for us, that he might redeem us from all lawlessness and purify for himself a people for his own possession, zealous for good works" |

**Analysis:** (1) EMET uses "on-behalf-of" (ΥΠΕΡ); LOGOS/PNEUMA use "for." (2) ΛΥΤΡΩΣΗΤΑΙ: EMET/LOGOS render "redeem"; PNEUMA renders "set us free." (3) ΠΕΡΙΟΥΣΙΟΝ: EMET has "own-possession"; LOGOS has "his own possession"; PNEUMA has "his very own." (4) ΖΗΛΩΤΗΝ ΚΑΛΩΝ ΕΡΓΩΝ: EMET has "zealous of good works"; LOGOS has "zealous for good works"; PNEUMA has "eager to do what is good." SOPHIA follows LOGOS throughout.

---

### Verse 15
| Translator | Rendering |
|---|---|
| EMET | "These things speak and exhort and reprove with all authority. Let no one despise you." |
| LOGOS | "Speak these things, and exhort and rebuke with all authority. Let no one despise you." |
| PNEUMA | "Teach these things. Encourage and correct with full authority. Do not let anyone look down on you." |
| **SOPHIA** | "Speak these things, and exhort and rebuke with all authority. Let no one despise you." |

**Analysis:** (1) ΛΑΛΕΙ: EMET/LOGOS render "speak"; PNEUMA renders "teach." (2) ΕΛΕΓΧΕ: EMET has "reprove," LOGOS has "rebuke," PNEUMA has "correct." (3) ΠΑΡΑΚΑΛΕΙ: EMET has "exhort," LOGOS has "exhort," PNEUMA has "encourage." (4) ΠΕΡΙΦΡΟΝΕΙΤΩ: EMET/LOGOS render "despise"; PNEUMA renders "look down on." (5) PNEUMA breaks the verse into three separate sentences and uses "full" rather than "all" for ΠΑΣΗΣ. SOPHIA follows LOGOS.

---

*Of the 15 verses in Titus 2, fourteen showed meaningful divergences (verses 1-5, 7-15). Only verse 6 achieved essential agreement across all three translators.*

--- SOPHIA
